paragraphs: Perceptual constancy is the tendency of the visual system to perceive objects as stable in size, shape, and color despite continual changes in the image cast upon the retina. Every time an object moves or the light shifts, the raw signal reaching the eye is altered, yet the world appears reassuringly fixed. This stability is not given by the eye itself but is constructed by the brain from incomplete information.

This construction depends on the brain's use of context. To gauge size, the visual system factors in how far away an object is judged to be, scaling the retinal image accordingly. [■] Color constancy works similarly, as the brain discounts the tint of the prevailing light so that a red apple appears red under both sunlight and lamplight. [■] Shape constancy, in turn, allows a door to seem rectangular even as it swings and casts a slanted trapezoid onto the retina. [■]

These principles matter well beyond the laboratory. [■] Because the brain reads size and distance together, designers can manipulate one to suggest the other. Architects now apply these findings when they taper columns or narrow a corridor to make a hall feel longer and grander than its true dimensions.

Every time an object moves or the light shifts, the raw signal reaching the eye is altered, yet the world appears reassuringly fixed. This stability is not given by the eye itself but is constructed by the brain from incomplete information. This construction depends on the brain's use of context. To gauge size, the visual system factors in how far away an object is judged to be, scaling the retinal image accordingly. [■] Color constancy works similarly, as the brain discounts the tint of the prevailing light so that a red apple appears red under both sunlight and lamplight. [■] Shape constancy, in turn, allows a door to seem rectangular even as it swings and casts a slanted trapezoid onto the retina. [■] These principles matter well beyond the laboratory. [■] Because the brain reads size and distance together, designers can manipulate one to suggest the other. Architects now apply these findings when they taper columns or narrow a corridor to make a hall feel longer and grander than its true dimensions.

questions.stem: The word "discounts" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: measures
options.B: reflects
options.C: disregards
options.D: intensifies

questions.explanation: The brain "discounts the tint of the prevailing light" so an apple looks red in any light, meaning it disregards or sets aside that tint.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does the visual system keep an object's size constant?

options.A: By discounting the tint of the surrounding light
options.B: By comparing the object with other nearby objects of known size
options.C: By focusing both eyes on the object's outer edges
options.D: By factoring in how far away the object is judged to be

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states the system "factors in how far away an object is judged to be, scaling the retinal image accordingly."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about the image on the retina?

options.A: It stays constant as long as the object itself does not move
options.B: It is always sharper at its center than at its edges
options.C: It gives the brain complete information about every object
options.D: It alone cannot account for the stable appearance of objects

questions.explanation: The passage says the retinal signal "is altered" yet the world stays fixed because stability is "constructed by the brain," implying the image alone is insufficient.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention a red apple in paragraph 2?

options.A: To show that some colors are easier for the eye to perceive than others
options.B: To illustrate how a color stays stable despite changes in lighting
options.C: To explain why ripe apples appear red rather than green
options.D: To demonstrate that lighting strongly alters an object's perceived color

questions.explanation: The apple that "appears red under both sunlight and lamplight" is a concrete example illustrating color constancy across lighting conditions.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, a car approaching from far away is judged to be full-sized, not tiny, even though its image on the retina is small.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete size-and-distance example, so it fits slot 1, immediately after the claim that the system scales the retinal image using judged distance.

paragraphs: A Roman aqueduct was a channel engineered to carry water across great distances by means of a gentle, continuous downward slope. Fed by mountain springs, it delivered a steady flow to the fountains, baths, and households of distant cities. The whole system relied not on pumps but on gravity, which drew the water forward wherever the ground descended even slightly.

This dependence on gravity forced surveyors to control the gradient with remarkable precision. [■] Over many kilometers the channel might drop only a few meters, so any error could halt the flow or let it overspill. Where valleys interrupted the route, engineers raised towering arcades of stone arches to keep the water at the proper height. [■] Where hills blocked the path, inverted siphons were used, sealed pipes that let pressure carry the water down one slope and up the next. [■]

The finished aqueducts reshaped urban life by making abundant clean water ordinary rather than rare. [■] Public baths, latrines, and drinking fountains all depended on their unbroken supply. Researchers are continuously uncovering new branches and measuring the mineral deposits inside the channels to learn how much water these systems actually carried.

Fed by mountain springs, it delivered a steady flow to the fountains, baths, and households of distant cities. The whole system relied not on pumps but on gravity, which drew the water forward wherever the ground descended even slightly. This dependence on gravity forced surveyors to control the gradient with remarkable precision. [■] Over many kilometers the channel might drop only a few meters, so any error could halt the flow or let it overspill. Where valleys interrupted the route, engineers raised towering arcades of stone arches to keep the water at the proper height. [■] Where hills blocked the path, inverted siphons were used, sealed pipes that let pressure carry the water down one slope and up the next. [■] The finished aqueducts reshaped urban life by making abundant clean water ordinary rather than rare. [■] Public baths, latrines, and drinking fountains all depended on their unbroken supply. Researchers are continuously uncovering new branches and measuring the mineral deposits inside the channels to learn how much water these systems actually carried.

questions.stem: The word "halt" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: measure
options.B: speed
options.C: stop
options.D: clean

questions.explanation: A gradient error "could halt the flow or let it overspill," so "halt" means to stop the water's movement.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how did engineers carry water across valleys?

options.A: By using sealed inverted siphons under pressure
options.B: By cutting long tunnels through the valley floor
options.C: By installing mechanical pumps at regular intervals
options.D: By raising tall arcades of stone arches

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says that "where valleys interrupted the route, engineers raised towering arcades of stone arches to keep the water at the proper height."

questions.stem: What does "their" refer to in paragraph 3 ("all depended on their unbroken supply")?

options.A: the aqueducts
options.B: public baths
options.C: drinking fountains
options.D: mineral deposits

questions.explanation: The "unbroken supply" is the water delivered by the aqueducts, so "their" refers back to the finished aqueducts named at the start of the paragraph.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about a city's water source?

options.A: Cities were usually built above the springs that fed them
options.B: A water source had to lie higher in elevation than the city
options.C: Most cities relied mainly on collected rainwater
options.D: Pumps were required wherever the ground lay flat

questions.explanation: Because the system used only gravity and needed ground that "descended even slightly," the source had to sit higher than the city it supplied.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, some channels fell less than the height of a person over the length of an entire day's march.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete measure of the tiny gradient, so it fits slot 1, right after the claim that surveyors controlled the gradient with great precision.

paragraphs: A nebula is an immense cloud of gas and dust scattered through the space between the stars. Composed mainly of hydrogen, these clouds can stretch across many light-years yet remain thinner than any vacuum produced on Earth. Despite this near-emptiness, a nebula holds enough raw material to build thousands of stars.

This star-building potential is released when part of a nebula grows dense enough to collapse under its own gravity. Such collapse is often triggered by a nearby shock wave, perhaps from an exploding star, that squeezes the gas past a critical density. As a clump contracts, it heats up and spins faster, eventually forming a hot core that will become a star. The surrounding gas, meanwhile, flattens into a rotating disk from which planets may later assemble.

Astronomers study these regions by observing the light that passes through or radiates from the dust. Infrared telescopes are especially useful, because their wavelengths pierce the dusty veil that hides newborn stars from ordinary view. Together these observations reveal a nebula not as an empty smudge but as a nursery where gas, gravity, and time convert diffuse matter into stars and worlds.
items.passage: A nebula is an immense cloud of gas and dust scattered through the space between the stars. Composed mainly of hydrogen, these clouds can stretch across many light-years yet remain thinner than any vacuum produced on Earth. Despite this near-emptiness, a nebula holds enough raw material to build thousands of stars. This star-building potential is released when part of a nebula grows dense enough to collapse under its own gravity. Such collapse is often triggered by a nearby shock wave, perhaps from an exploding star, that squeezes the gas past a critical density. As a clump contracts, it heats up and spins faster, eventually forming a hot core that will become a star. The surrounding gas, meanwhile, flattens into a rotating disk from which planets may later assemble. Astronomers study these regions by observing the light that passes through or radiates from the dust. Infrared telescopes are especially useful, because their wavelengths pierce the dusty veil that hides newborn stars from ordinary view. Together these observations reveal a nebula not as an empty smudge but as a nursery where gas, gravity, and time convert diffuse matter into stars and worlds.

questions.stem: The word "pierce" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: penetrate
options.B: reflect
options.C: scatter
options.D: brighten

questions.explanation: Infrared wavelengths "pierce the dusty veil" that hides new stars, meaning they penetrate or pass through the dust.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens to a clump of gas as it contracts?

options.A: It cools down and gradually stops rotating
options.B: It splits into two nearly equal halves
options.C: It releases most of its stored hydrogen back into space
options.D: It heats up and spins faster, forming a hot core

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says that "as a clump contracts, it heats up and spins faster, eventually forming a hot core that will become a star."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about nebulae?

options.A: They are denser than most substances found on Earth
options.B: Their vast size, not their density, gives them so much material
options.C: They are made of equal parts hydrogen and solid dust
options.D: They contain far more matter than all the stars they form

questions.explanation: Since a nebula is "thinner than any vacuum" yet holds material for thousands of stars, its enormous extent, rather than its density, accounts for that material.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention infrared telescopes in paragraph 3?

options.A: To argue that ordinary optical telescopes are now obsolete
options.B: To show that dust makes newborn stars completely impossible to study
options.C: To explain how stars hidden by dust can still be observed
options.D: To describe the exact wavelengths of ordinary visible starlight

questions.explanation: Infrared wavelengths "pierce the dusty veil that hides newborn stars," so the mention explains how astronomers observe stars concealed by dust.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Nebulae are denser than a laboratory vacuum on Earth
options.B: Nebulae are composed mainly of hydrogen gas
options.C: A collapsing clump can leave a rotating disk where planets form
options.D: Infrared wavelengths can pass through nebular dust

questions.explanation: The passage says nebulae "remain thinner than any vacuum produced on Earth," so calling them denser than a laboratory vacuum is false.

paragraphs: Water treatment is the set of chemical and physical processes that make raw water safe for people to drink. Water drawn from rivers and lakes carries suspended clay, dissolved minerals, and living microbes that simple straining cannot remove. Because the finest clay particles are far too small to be caught by an ordinary filter, cleaning such water demands a chemical step rather than a purely mechanical one.

This chemical step, known as coagulation, solves the problem of tiny suspended particles. Treatment plants add coagulants such as aluminum salts, whose positive charges neutralize the negative charges that keep clay particles apart. Freed from this mutual repulsion, the particles collide and clump into larger clusters called flocs, which grow heavy enough to settle out of the water.

These settled flocs are then filtered off, and the clarified water is disinfected to kill any remaining microbes. This combined approach has sharply reduced waterborne disease in cities that can afford it. Some dissolved chemicals still slip through, and disinfection itself may form unwanted byproducts, yet ongoing research into membranes and advanced oxidation promises even more sophisticated solutions.
items.passage: Water treatment is the set of chemical and physical processes that make raw water safe for people to drink. Water drawn from rivers and lakes carries suspended clay, dissolved minerals, and living microbes that simple straining cannot remove. Because the finest clay particles are far too small to be caught by an ordinary filter, cleaning such water demands a chemical step rather than a purely mechanical one. This chemical step, known as coagulation, solves the problem of tiny suspended particles. Treatment plants add coagulants such as aluminum salts, whose positive charges neutralize the negative charges that keep clay particles apart. Freed from this mutual repulsion, the particles collide and clump into larger clusters called flocs, which grow heavy enough to settle out of the water. These settled flocs are then filtered off, and the clarified water is disinfected to kill any remaining microbes. This combined approach has sharply reduced waterborne disease in cities that can afford it. Some dissolved chemicals still slip through, and disinfection itself may form unwanted byproducts, yet ongoing research into membranes and advanced oxidation promises even more sophisticated solutions.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The way aluminum salts dissolve when added to river water
options.B: The many kinds of microbes found in lakes and rivers
options.C: How chemical treatment removes particles to make water drinkable
options.D: Why mechanical filters alone are the best way to purify water

questions.explanation: The passage moves from the problem of unfilterable particles to coagulation and disinfection, so it is mainly about making raw water safe through chemical treatment.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what do the added coagulants do to the clay particles?

options.A: They dissolve the clay particles completely
options.B: They neutralize the charges that keep the particles apart
options.C: They coat each particle with a protective film
options.D: They raise the temperature of the surrounding raw water

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says coagulants have "positive charges" that "neutralize the negative charges that keep clay particles apart," letting them clump.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It presents a solution to the problem described in paragraph 1
options.B: It gives historical background for the claims in paragraph 1
options.C: It contradicts the central argument made in paragraph 1
options.D: It provides statistical evidence supporting paragraph 1

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 sets up the problem that filters cannot catch fine particles, and paragraph 2 introduces coagulation as the chemical solution.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about water treatment?

options.A: It removes every dissolved chemical from the water
options.B: It is now available to every city in the world
options.C: It relies mainly on boiling the water to kill microbes
options.D: It leaves some dissolved contaminants in the treated water

questions.explanation: The passage notes that "some dissolved chemicals still slip through," implying treated water can retain certain contaminants.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what happens to the flocs after they form?

options.A: They are dissolved by adding still more coagulant
options.B: They are collected and later reused as fertilizer
options.C: They are heated until the water around them evaporates
options.D: They are filtered out before the water is disinfected

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says "these settled flocs are then filtered off, and the clarified water is disinfected."
